coredata.dc:title: Rapid determination of dopamine in human plasma using a gold nanoparticle-based dual-mode sensing system
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yali
Qi, Suijian
Liu, Zhonggang
Shi, Yupeng
Yue, Wanqing
Yi, Changqing
coredata.dc:description: Dopamine plays a very important role in biological systems and has a direct relationship with the ability of learning and cognition, human desires, feelings and mental state, as well as motor functions. Traditional methods for the detection of dopamine are complicated and time-consuming, therefore it is necessary to explore rapid and accurate detection of dopamine with high sensitivity and specificity. Herein we report a dual-mode system of colorimetric and fluorometric analyses based on gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and aptamers specifically targeting dopamine. Aptamers modified with the fluorophore were used as dopamine specific recognition probe and the sensing mechanism is based on the color change of AuNPs and the fluorescence recovery of fluorophore conjugated on the aptamers in the presence of dopamine. The addition of aptamers into AuNPs colloid solution would prevent the AuNPs from aggregation in the high-salt solution. The close distance between AuNPs and fluorophore conjugated on the aptamers would lead to the quenching of fluorescence signal. In the presence of dopamine, the conformation of the aptamers and the inter-particle distance would be changed, leading to the aggregation of AuNPs, which subsequently results in color change from red to blue and fluorescence signal recovery. The dual-mode sensing system demonstrated high specificity towards dopamine with the detection limit as low as 78.7nM. The sensing system reflects on its simplicity as no surface functionalization is required for the nanoparticles, leading to less laborious and more cost-effective synthesis. The reaction time is only 6min, demonstrating a simple approach for rapid analysis of dopamine. More importantly, the sensing system allows the detection of dopamine in both aqueous solution and complicated biological sample with sensitive response, illustrating the feasibility and reliability for the potential applications in clinical and biomedical analysis in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dopamine
Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)
Aptamers
Colorimetric detection
Fluorometric detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of dopamine using the dual-mode sensing system
Detection of dopamine in human plasma
Determination of dopamine in human plasma

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mechanism for dopamine detection using FAM-labeled aptamers and AuNPs.

(A) TEM images of AuNPs in the absence of NaCl. (B) TEM images of AuNPs in the presence of 31.25mM NaCl. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of AuNP solution containing 31.25mM NaCl and 167nM dopamine specific binding aptamers and non-specific aptamers (control) in the presence of 1.0 and 1.5μM dopamine. Inset: photographic images of the AuNPs solution under different conditions. (D) Overlay chart of the UV–vis absorption spectrum of AuNPs and the fluorescence spectrum of FAM-labeled aptamer.

(A) The absorbance ratios of 635nm/524nm (A 635/A 524) after the addition of 0–80mM NaCl in 600μL solution containing 3.77nM AuNPs. (B) The absorbance ratios of 635nm/524nm (A 635/A 524) after the addition of 0–200nM aptamer in 600μL solution containing 31.25mM NaCl and 3.77nM AuNPs. (C) The time-dependent absorbance ratios of 635nm/524nm (A 635/A 524) of the 600μL solution containing 167nM aptamer, 31.25mM NaCl and 3.77nM AuNPs.

(A) Photographic images of the AuNPs solution in the presence of 31.25mM NaCl and 167nM aptamer under different concentrations of dopamine. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of AuNPs in presence of 31.25mM NaCl and 167nM aptamer under different concentrations of dopamine. (C) The absorption ratio (A 635/A 524) responses with the addition of 0–10.0μM dopamine. Inset: calibration of A 635/A 524 vs. dopamine concentration in the AuNPs solution.

(A) Fluorescence spectrum of AuNPs in presence of 31.25mM NaCl and 167nM aptamer under different concentrations of dopamine. (B) Fluorescence intensity responses with the addition of 0–2.5μM dopamine. Inset: calibration of fluorescence intensity vs. dopamine concentration in the AuNPs solution.

(A) The absorption ratio (A 635/A 524) of the dual-mode sensing system in the presence of hydroquinone, glucose, ascorbic acid, l-phenylalanine, l-tryptophan, norepinephrine, serotonin hydrochloride, 3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine and dopamine. (B) The relative fluorescent intensity of the dual-mode sensing system in the presence of hydroquinone, glucose, ascorbic acid, l-phenylalanine, l-tryptophan, norepinephrine, serotonin hydrochloride, 3,4-dihydroxy-phenylalanine and dopamine.
Comparison of different optical sensing systems for dopamine determination.
Analytical results of dopamine in spiked human plasma samples.

head.ce:title: Rapid determination of dopamine in human plasma using a gold nanoparticle-based dual-mode sensing system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Sensing Technology and Biomedical Instruments (Guangdong Province), School of Engineering, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Sensing Technology and Biomedical Instruments (Guangdong Province)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Light Industry and Food Sciences, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Light Industry and Food Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano and Advanced Materials Institute Limited, Hong Kong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nano and Advanced Materials Institute Limited

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dopamine plays a very important role in biological systems and has a direct relationship with the ability of learning and cognition, human desires, feelings and mental state, as well as motor functions. Traditional methods for the detection of dopamine are complicated and time-consuming, therefore it is necessary to explore rapid and accurate detection of dopamine with high sensitivity and specificity. Herein we report a dual-mode system of colorimetric and fluorometric analyses based on gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and aptamers specifically targeting dopamine. Aptamers modified with the fluorophore were used as dopamine specific recognition probe and the sensing mechanism is based on the color change of AuNPs and the fluorescence recovery of fluorophore conjugated on the aptamers in the presence of dopamine. The addition of aptamers into AuNPs colloid solution would prevent the AuNPs from aggregation in the high-salt solution. The close distance between AuNPs and fluorophore conjugated on the aptamers would lead to the quenching of fluorescence signal. In the presence of dopamine, the conformation of the aptamers and the inter-particle distance would be changed, leading to the aggregation of AuNPs, which subsequently results in color change from red to blue and fluorescence signal recovery. The dual-mode sensing system demonstrated high specificity towards dopamine with the detection limit as low as 78.7nM. The sensing system reflects on its simplicity as no surface functionalization is required for the nanoparticles, leading to less laborious and more cost-effective synthesis. The reaction time is only 6min, demonstrating a simple approach for rapid analysis of dopamine. More importantly, the sensing system allows the detection of dopamine in both aqueous solution and complicated biological sample with sensitive response, illustrating the feasibility and reliability for the potential applications in clinical and biomedical analysis in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine, one of the small molecules in the monoaminergic neuroreceptor family [1], has been widely studied since it was discovered [2]. Dopamine plays key roles in central nervous systems and serves many functions in dopaminergic neuron process. The disorder levels of dopamine may lead to many neurological diseases such as sleeping and eating disorders, Parkinson's disease, addictive behaviors combined with drug abuse, schizophrenia, and Alzheimer's disease [3–5]. Meanwhile, it is unlawful to use dopamine as a new alternative of “Clenbuterol” as the residues of dopamine in livestock are harmful to human, which presents a challenge for food safety monitoring. Therefore, methods for rapid and sensitive detection of dopamine in biological system are very important for neurological disorder diagnosis [6] and food safety monitoring. Conventional methods for determining dopamine include high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7], mass spectroscopy [8], electrochemical analysis [9,10], chemiluminescence [11], enzyme linked immunosorbent assay [12], surface plasmonic detection [13] and so on. However, most of these methods are time-consuming and require sophisticated pre-treatment process, expensive instruments and expertise for operation. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a new sensing system for rapid detection of dopamine with high sensitivity and specificity. Due to its simplicity and practicability with good sensitivity, electrochemical and optical techniques have been extensively explored for developing dopamine sensors. Electrochemical sensors are the most straightforward, rapid, and cost-effective way for dopamine detection. In order to improve their performance, many new materials, for example conducting polymers, ionic liquids, and nanomaterials have been used for sensor construction [9,10,14,15]. Especially, nanomaterials have been demonstrated as excellent sensing components due to their unique physical and chemical properties, which have been widely applied in analytical chemistry and biomedical diagnosis [14–17]. To circumvent the overvoltage and slow kinetics of the electrode process, metal nanoparticles (Au, Ag, Pt, Pd, Cu) are commonly used as active materials to modify the working electrodes for enhancing the sensitivity and selectivity of electrochemical dopamine sensors [14]. For example, the electrode modified with Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) which have been decorated with polypyrrole/reduced graphene oxide hybrid sheets showed the lowest detection limit of 18.29pM and also demonstrated the good analytical performance in real samples such as human serum and urine [9]. However, determination of dopamine at the nanomolar level characteristic of living system is still challenging for electrochemical sensors in the presence of dominant interferents [10].

Optical sensing incorporated with nanoparticles as signal generators has been proven as the most convenient and comprehensible method. Several colorimetric methods based on the distance-dependent optical properties of AuNPs have been developed for dopamine detection. The color variation of AuNPs associated with the reversible process from dispersion to aggregation induced by the intermolecular interactions between dopamine and surface modifiers on the AuNPs can be observed by naked eyes and quantified by UV–vis spectra [18–22]. In addition, several fluorometric assays have been demonstrated for the detection of dopamine with high sensitivity using fluorophores such as calcein blue–Fe2 + complex [23], carbon dots [24], silicon nanoparticles [25], molecule capped QDs [26–29], and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) [30,31]. To the best of our knowledge, only one dual-mode assay has been reported for dopamine detection, where BSA-stabilized AuNCs were employed as sensing components [32]. However, the instability of AuNCs hinders the wide application of the assay.

In this report, a dual-mode sensing system with both colorimetric and fluorometric readouts for rapid detection of dopamine has been developed. Our proposed dual-mode sensing system consists of AuNPs and dopamine-binding aptamers. In the colorimetric mode, dopamine could specifically bind to the aptamers thus changing the stability of the AuNPs in solution, resulting in the color change of AuNPs solution. In the fluorometric mode, a fluorescence resonance energy transfer system (FRET) is developed using the fluorophore labeled aptamers as donors and AuNPs as acceptors [33–37]. The dual-mode sensing system has high specificity towards dopamine with the detection limit of as low as 78.7nM. Compared with conventional methods, the advantages of the dual-mode sensing system include its simplicity, rapid analysis, comprehensible qualitative and quantitative results and robust in completed biological sample. As no surface functionalization is required for the nanoparticles, the simplicity of the probe preparation leads to less laborious and more cost-effective synthesis. The reaction time is only 6min and the results could be visualized by naked eyes and quantified by spectrophotometer, which demonstrates the sensing system as a robust and simple approach for rapid analysis of dopamine. More importantly, the sensing system allows the detection of dopamine in both aqueous solution and complicated biological sample with sensitive response, which could be potentially applied in clinical diagnosis and food safety monitoring in the future.

Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) tetrahydrate, trisodium citrate, dopamine, norepinephrine, hydroquinone, ascorbic acid, homocysteine, l-tryptophan, l-phenylalanine, 5-hydroxytryptamine, 3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine, glucose, and uric acid were all of analytical reagent grade and purchased from J&K (Beijing, China). Two 58-mer oligonucleotides strands were synthesized by Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China): one strand of oligonucleotides with the base sequences of 5′-GTC TCT GTG TGC GCC AGA GAA CAC TGG GGC AGA TAT GGG CCA GCA CAG AAT GAG GCC C-3′6-FAM [18] was designed specifically for capture of dopamine and the other strand of oligonucleotides was used as control with the sequences of 5′-ACG GTT GCA AGT GGG ACT CTG GTA GGC TGG GTT AAT TTG G-3′. The single stranded DNA was dissolved in 100mM phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH=7.0) before use. Other solutions were prepared by ultra-pure water (Milli-Q plus, Millipore Inc., Bedford, MA).

Morphology of the synthesized nanoparticles was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM 1400). UV–vis spectra of the nanoparticles were obtained by a Beckman DU370 UV–vis spectrometer and fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Fluoromax-4P spectrometer (Horiba).

Aqueous dispersions of citrate-stabilized AuNPs were prepared according to the published protocol [18]: 120mL of 1mM HAuCl4 solution was heated to boiling with vigorous stirring in the round-bottom flask. 12mL of 38.8mM trisodium citrate solution was rapidly added into the solution and the reaction continued for another 15–20min. After that, the solution was cooled down to room temperature and filtered by a 0.45μm cellulose acetate (CA) membrane. The prepared AuNPs were stored at 4°C in the refrigerator for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of dopamine using the dual-mode sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: 200μL of dopamine with different concentrations was mixed with 10μL of 10μM aptamers in 1.5mL plastic tubes, respectively. After incubation for 5min, 200μL of 11.3nM AuNPs was added and reacted for another 5min in solution. Then 75μL of 0.25M NaCl was rapidly added into the vial and incubated for another 6min. 30mM PBS buffer was used to keep the total volume of 600μL before spectrum measurement. For colorimetric readout, 130μL of the mixture was transferred to a 1cm path length quartz cuvette for UV–vis measurement. For fluorometric readout, 300μL of the mixture was transferred to cuvette for recording emission spectra.

To evaluate the selectivity of the dual-mode system, 30μL of 100μM of different kinds of interferents including hydroquinone (HQ), glucose (Glu), ascorbic acid (AA), l-phenylalanine (l-Phe), l-tryptophan (l-Try), norepinephrine (NE), serotonin hydrochloride (5-HT), and 3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanin (DOPA) was mixed firstly with 10μL of 10μM dopamine-binding aptamers respectively and incubated for 5min. Then 200μL of 11.3nM AuNPs was added and reacted for 5min, followed by the rapidaddition of 75μL of 0.25M NaCl into the vial and incubated for another 6min. 300μL of the mixture was transferred for fluorescence measurement and 130μL of the mixture was transferred for colorimetric measurement. 30mM PBS buffer was used to keep the total volume of 600μL before spectrum measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of dopamine in human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of dopamine was carried out in deproteinized human plasma. The plasma proteins were precipitated using acetonitrile (two thirds of the reconstitution volume) by centrifugation at 4000rpm for 30min [38]. The supernatant was collected and 1000 times diluted with ultra-pure water before use. Different concentrations of dopamine were spiked into the deproteinized blank human plasma and quantified by the present sensing system with dual-mode readouts. The recovery rate was calculated accordingly and compared with HPLC results.

The dual-mode (colorimetric mode and fluorometric mode) sensing system (Fig. 1 ) consists of fluorophore-labeled dopamine-binding aptamers and AuNPs. It is well known that AuNPs possess distance-dependent optical properties and have a superior quenching efficiency [33–37]. The addition of aptamers into AuNPs colloid solution would prevent the AuNPs from aggregation in the high-salt solution while the close distance between AuNPs and fluorophore conjugated on the aptamers leads to the fluorescence quenching. In the presence of dopamine, the conformation of the aptamers would be changed and thus the inter-particle distance would be enlarged, leading to the aggregation of AuNPs, which subsequently results in color change from red to blue and recovery of the fluorescent signal.

In the present report, the diameter of the synthesized AuNPs was ~13nm (Fig. 2A) and the concentration of the AuNPs solution was calculated to be 11.3nM using the extinction coefficient (2.7×108 M−1 cm−1) at 520nm according to Beer's law [39]. Before the measurement, AuNPs solution in red was stabilized by the surface citrate anions (Fig. 2A). When adding a high concentration of salt such as NaCl solution, the electrostatic balance among nanoparticles was destroyed, such that AuNPs were aggregated (Fig. 2B) with the color change from red to blue. However, it has been reported that aptamers with a random coil structure could be adsorbed onto the surface of AuNPs through the coordination interaction between the nitrogen atoms on the exposed bases and the AuNPs [40–42]. The negative charged aptamers on the surface of AuNPs could prevent AuNPs from NaCl-induced aggregation to enhance the stability, resulting in dispersed AuNPs solution in red (Fig. 2C, control). As dopamine could specifically bind to the dopamine-binding aptamers, the presence of dopamine could induce the conformation switching of aptamers from a random flexible structure to a rigid stem–loop structure. The bases on the dopamine bond aptamers with rigid stem–loop structures were not exposed outside, such that the dopamine bond aptamers could not be adsorbed onto the AuNPs to protect AuNPs from NaCl-induced aggregation. Therefore, in the presence of both aptamers and dopamine, AuNPs would aggregate and the solution would turn blue, which was verified by the color change of the solution, and the decrement in the absorbance at 524nm while the increment in absorbance at 635nm (Fig. 2C). The colorimetric measurement was established accordingly by the color change of AuNPs from red to blue, which can be observed by naked eyes and quantified by UV–vis spectra.

In the fluorometric mode, a FRET system is developed using the fluorophore labeled aptamers as donors and AuNPs as acceptors. In the presence of aptamers with a random coil structure, AuNPs in the NaCl solution were kept dispersed and the close distance between aptamers and AuNP allows the fluorescence resonance energy transfer from the fluorophore conjugated on the aptamers to the AuNPs. As shown in Fig. 2D, an obvious spectral overlap between 6-carboxyfluorescein (FAM) and AuNPs was observed, suggesting that AuNPs could successfully quench the fluorescence of FAM in close distance. With the addition of dopamine into AuNPs solution, the specific binding of dopamine to aptamers changed the conformation of the aptamers, thus aptamers would not be adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs, resulting in longer distance between AuNPs and the FAM. The FRET efficiency was decreased due to the longer distance between the fluorescent donor (FAM) and acceptor (AuNPs), such that the fluorescence of the FAM was recovered. The fluorometric readout was established by measuring the recovery of the FAM fluorescence signal.

In the presence of dopamine-binding aptamers and dopamine, two absorption peaks were showed in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 2C). One characteristic surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak is at 524nm, and the other peak center is at 635nm, ascribing to the absorption from the aggregated AuNPs [43–47]. With the increment of concentrations of dopamine in the mixture, i.e. in the presence of aptamer and 1.0μM dopamine and 1.5μM dopamine as shown in Fig. 2C, the absorbance at 524nm decreased while the absorbance at 635nm increased, which is consistent with the color change of AuNPs solution. A lower ratio was associated with the dispersed AuNPs with red color, while a higher ratio corresponded to the aggregated AuNPs with blue color [48]. Therefore, we used the ratio of the absorbance at 635nm (A 635) to that at 524nm (A 524) which is consistent with the color change of AuNPs solution to optimize the experimental conditions.

The three main factors that would affect the absorbance ratio are the concentration of NaCl, the concentration of aptamers and the reaction time after the addition of NaCl. NaCl addition caps the repulsion between negatively unmodified AuNPs, leading to the aggregation of AuNPs. It has been reported previously that 90mM NaCl can cause aggregation of DNA-protected AuNPs, possibly because the DNA internal charges are screened to allow for more compact structures on the AuNP surface with high salt, resulting in the reduced number of contacting points and thus facilitating desorption [53]. Firstly, we studied the influence of the concentration of NaCl in the range of 0–80mM and found that the absorbance ratio increased with the increment of the NaCl concentration and reached to a plateau after the concentration of NaCl higher than 31.25mM, which was considered as the optimized concentration of NaCl (Fig. 3A).

Since the absorbed aptamers can create an exclusion zone to other incoming DNA, further adsorption of more DNA is hindered once the electrostatic exclusion zones start to overlap. It is understandable that the electrostatic exclusion is weaker and the protection for AuNPs is obvious, when the concentration of aptamer is lower. With the increase of aptamer, the electrostatic exclusion enhances and the absorbed DNA desorbs [53]. Therefore, the optimal concentration of aptamers was studied in the range of 0–200nM. As shown in Fig. 3B, the absorbance ratio reached a plateau when the concentration of aptamers was 167nM, which was thus selected as the economy and optimum concentration of aptamers.

Finally, we optimized the reaction time for NaCl and AuNPs in the range of 0–30min. It was showed that the ratio increased quickly in the first 6min. Therefore, 6min was chosen to be the optimal reaction time. The optimal conditions were used in the following experiments to determine the sensitivity and specificity of the nanosensing system.

The color change of the AuNPs solution could be visualized by the naked eye (Fig. 4A) and quantified by the UV–vis spectroscopy. The ratio of the absorbance at 635nm (A 635) to that at 524nm (A 524) was used to characterize the color change caused by dopamine with different concentrations (Fig. 4B). As shown in the inset of Fig. 4C, the ratio of A 635 to A 524 increased proportionally with the concentration of 0.17–4.0μM with a linear equation A635/A524 =0.0807CDA +0.179 (R 2 =0.9975). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as low as 0.14μM, according to the standard deviation of the response (SD) and the slope of the calibration curve (S) at levels approximating the LOD with the formula: LOD=3.3 (SD/S).

The concentration of dopamine could be also quantified by the fluorescent intensity of the solution at the wavelength of 515nm, which is the maximum emission wavelength of the FAM labeled on the aptamers. As showed in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of 515nm (F) increased gradually with the increase of the dopamine concentration. The fluorescent intensity was quantified by calculating the intensity ratio (F−F0)/F0, where F is the fluorescence intensity of 515nm in different dopamine concentrations and F0 is the fluorescence intensity in the absence of dopamine. The ratio of (F−F0)/F0 increased linearly in dopamine solution with the concentration in the range of 0.083 to 2.0μM with the equation (F−F0)/F0 =0.1386CDA +0.1011, R2 =0.998 (Fig. 5B). The limit of detection was calculated to be 78.7nM (inset of Fig. 5B). When compared with optical sensing systems reported in recent years (Table 1 ), our dual-mode sensing system provides better or comparable sensitivity for dopamine determination.

We have demonstrated that the dopamine-binding aptamers had a strong affinity to dopamine, and the sensing system showed great response to dopamine which could be discriminated by visual inspection and quantified by absorption and fluorescence intensity. Specificity is another important characteristic of a good sensing system as many interferents in the real sample would affect the accuracy of the sensing system. We evaluated the selectivity of the sensing system by measuring the absorption ratio of A 635/A 524 for different interferential substances including hydroquinone, glucose, ascorbic acid, l-phenylalanine, l-tryptophan, uric acid, norepinephrine and 5-hydroxytryptamine and 3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine. As shown in Fig. 6 , upon the same condition, the absorption ratio of the sensing system in the presence of dopamine was significantly higher than interferents, demonstrating the good specificity of the sensing system towards dopamine. As for the fluorometric mode, the fluorescence recoveries of the sensing system in the presence of interferents are significantly lower than dopamine. The combination of the dual-mode would have a relatively higher selectivity for dopamine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of dopamine in human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate the analytical performance of the dual-mode nanosensing system in complicated biological samples, 0.25, 0.5 and 1.0μM of dopamine were spiked into the blank human plasma which were subjected to colorimetric and fluorometric determination by the present sensing system. HPLC experiments were also performed to measure the dopamine content in the same spiked human plasma sample as a reference method. As shown in Table 2 , dopamine concentration in the human plasma samples spiked with 0.5μM analyte was determined as 0.472±0.007, 0.492±0.018 and 0.508±0.026μM using the HPLC method, the colorimetric mode, and the fluorometric mode of the dual-mode sensing system, respectively. Using our dual-mode sensing system, the recoveries in the colorimetric mode were 98.4% and 97.9% with RSD around 3%, and the recoveries in the fluorometric mode were 108.4% and 101.6% with RSD around 6%, respectively. Results from a paired Student's t-test suggested that there was no systematic difference between the dual-mode sensing system and HPLC method, indicating the good reliability of the sensing system for the detection of dopamine in the complicated biological samples.

In summary, based on the color change of AuNPs and FRET phenomenon, we have developed a dual-mode sensing system with both colorimetric and fluorometric readouts for the quantitative analysis of dopamine under physiological conditions. The sensing system described in this work reflects on its simplicity and flexibility, where no further surface functionalization is required for the prepared nanoparticles, leading to less laborious and more cost-effective synthesis. Compared to other methods, the detection time of the sensing system is only 6min, offering an efficient way for the rapid detection of dopamine. The proposed sensing system has a good dynamic range and a relatively low detection limit of 78.7nM. Good specificity to dopamine was also verified in the presence of competitive molecules such as hydroquinone, glucose and ascorbic acid. More importantly, the sensing system could not only perform in PBS buffer with high sensitivity but also exhibit sensitive responses toward dopamine in complicated biological environments. The potential application of the present sensing system in bioanalysis might be significant in disease diagnosis and food safety monitoring in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the Pearl River S&T Nova Program of Guangzhou (2013J2200053), Tip-top Scientific and Technical Innovative Youth Talents of Guangdong Special Support Program (2014TQ01R417), and Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (2014A030310282) is gratefully acknowledged.
